summary:
Land reform was one of the first and most consequential acts of the People's Republic of China after the Communist victory in 1949. The Land Reform Law of 1950 authorized the confiscation of land from landlords and its redistribution to the rural poor. The process was implemented through village-level struggle sessions in which peasants publicly denounced landlords, who were then classified and subjected to varying fates: some were executed, others imprisoned or subjected to labor, and some merely stripped of their land.

Estimates of landlords and wealthy peasants killed during the land reform process range from hundreds of thousands to several million. By 1953, the traditional landlord class had been effectively eliminated as a social category and approximately 45 million hectares of land had been redistributed to some 300 million poor and landless peasants. The reform destroyed the old rural social order and created a massive base of peasant support for the new regime. However, within a few years the [[event:2669|Communist government]] would move to collectivize agriculture, first into cooperatives and then into communes, eventually reversing private peasant ownership through the programs associated with the [[event:2705|Great Leap Forward]].

read_more_links.title: Agrarian Reform Law of the People's Republic of China
